1: Chapter 4 introduces the sin offering (ḥatta't) — a distinct offering type for dealing with specific categories of sin. Unlike the burnt offering (general atonement and dedication) and fellowship offering (celebration/peace), the sin offering addresses the concrete situation of having violated a specific command. The detailed structure of Leviticus 4 — four social classes with different requirements — reflects both a theology of graduated responsibility and a practical graduated-cost system.
2: beshegagah: 'unintentionally.' The sin offering applies specifically to shegagah — inadvertent, unintentional, or unknowing violations of divine commands. The deliberate parallel category is yad rama (high-handed sin, Numbers 15:30-31), for which there was no sacrificial remedy: the person is cut off. This distinction between inadvertent and deliberate sin is fundamental to the entire sin-offering system. Most sins covered in the Torah's sin-offering laws are acts done in ignorance, oversight, or inadvertence — not willful rebellion.
3: The anointed priest's sin offering: a young bull without defect (par ben-baqar tamim). The priest requires the most expensive offering because his sin has broader communal consequences — le'ashmat ha'am (bringing guilt on the people). The priestly role creates a heightened moral responsibility: the mediator's failure affects the community he mediates for. This is why Hebrews emphasizes that Jesus, unlike Levitical high priests, had no sin requiring his own offering first (Hebrews 7:27).
4: The hand-laying and slaughter procedure is the same as for burnt offerings. The sin offering's slaughter before YHWH at the sanctuary entrance gives it full covenant-transaction status: the death of the animal before YHWH is not private but official, witnessed by YHWH.
5: The anointed priest himself carries the blood inside the tent of meeting — the sin offering for the priest penetrates deeper into the sanctuary than most offerings. This inner-sanctuary blood manipulation is reserved for the two classes of offerer who represent the whole community (priest and congregation). The carrying of blood inside the tent models the Yom Kippur procedure in miniature.
6: The blood is sprinkled seven times before YHWH in front of the inner veil (parokhet haqqodesh). The sevenfold sprinkling is the completion number — seven applications before the veil that separates the holy place from the most holy. The blood reaches the boundary of the most sacred space, but does not enter it (only on Yom Kippur does blood enter the holy of holies). The spatial penetration of the blood mirrors the seriousness of the offense requiring atonement.
7: Some blood is applied to the four horns of the incense altar inside the tent — the altar closest to the divine presence. The horns of the altar (qarnot hamizbeaḥ) are the projecting corners that received blood in sin-offering rituals. The horn application and blood-pouring at the base of the burnt-offering altar together cover the sanctuary's altar furniture comprehensively — both inner (incense) and outer (burnt offering) altars receive blood.
8: The fat removal procedure for the priestly sin offering is identical to the fellowship offering (entrail fat, kidneys, liver lobe). However, unlike the fellowship offering, the priest and offerer receive no portion — the entire sin-offering animal is disposed of differently. The fat portions still belong to YHWH and are burned on the altar.
9: The two kidneys and liver lobe — standard YHWH portions. The sin offering's fat portions are handled exactly like the fellowship offering's, demonstrating that YHWH's portion is consistent across offering types. The difference lies not in what goes to YHWH but in what happens to the rest of the animal.
10: The parallel with the fellowship offering's fat removal is explicit: ka'asher yuram mish-shor zevaḥ hashlamim (just as they are removed from the ox of the fellowship offering). This cross-reference within the law is Leviticus's version of legal shorthand: rather than repeating all the anatomical details, it cites the established procedure of Leviticus 3. Such internal legal cross-referencing indicates a coherent legal corpus, not a random collection of rules.
11: The hide, flesh, head, legs, entrails, and dung of the priestly sin-offering bull — everything except the fat portions burned on the altar — must be removed entirely from the sanctuary. The complete enumeration of body parts emphasizes totality: nothing of the animal's body remains in the camp. The priestly sin offering has no human beneficiary — not offerer, not priest, no one in the camp eats from it.
12: The carcass is taken to the ash disposal site outside the camp (el-shar la-deshen miḥuts lammaḥaneh) and burned there entirely. The outside-the-camp disposal is significant: the most sin-laden sacrifices go furthest from the center. The carcass of the sin offering for the priest or congregation carries concentrated moral weight that requires spatial removal from the community. Hebrews 13:11-12 exploits this: Jesus suffered outside the gate to sanctify the people through his own blood, fulfilling the type of the sin-offering burned outside the camp.
13: The second case: the entire congregation (kol-adat Yisrael) sins unintentionally. Communal sin is possible — a wrong done collectively without individual awareness. The example is a commandment hidden from the assembly (ne'lam min-ha'eda) — they have violated something without knowing it. This addresses the reality of collective moral oversight in community life, not merely individual transgression.
14: The communal sin offering is the same as the priestly sin offering: a young bull. The equivalence in offering between priest and congregation reflects their parallel representational roles — just as the priest's sin affects the whole community, so the community's collective sin requires the same level of atonement. The elders represent the congregation in the laying-on-of-hands and slaughter.
15: The ziqne ha'eda (elders of the congregation) lay their hands on the bull's head on behalf of the whole assembly — a representative semikah. The elders' identification act extends the individual hand-laying mechanism to the corporate level: the whole congregation is present through its representative leadership. This creates a theology of corporate representation in atonement.
16: The anointed priest brings the community sin-offering blood inside the tent — same procedure as for his own sin offering. The priest acts as mediator for communal sin as he does for his own: the same blood-manipulation procedure covers both cases. The repetition of the inner-sanctuary blood ritual for both priestly and communal sin offerings signals that both represent the most serious impurity requiring maximum penetration toward the divine presence.
17: The sevenfold sprinkling before the veil — exactly as in Leviticus 4:6. The parallel treatment of priestly and communal sin offerings is deliberate: both require the same level of atonement because both affect the entire covenant community. The priest's sin affects the people; the people's sin involves the priests. The two cases are mirror images.
18: Blood on the horns of the incense altar and poured out at the base of the burnt-offering altar — same as the priestly offering. The full altar coverage (inner incense altar and outer burnt-offering altar) is maintained for both community cases. The outside disposal of the carcass (Leviticus 4:11-12 and 21) completes the parallel — both priestly and communal sin offerings are burned outside the camp.
19: All fat portions are removed and burned on the altar — same as the priestly case. The consistency of procedure across the two community-level sin offerings (priest and congregation) reflects the legal principle that similar cases receive similar treatment. Leviticus 4 is organized by social rank but unified by procedural consistency within each rank-class.
20: vekhipper aleihem hakohen venislaḥ lahem: 'The priest makes atonement for them and they shall be forgiven.' The atonement formula of the sin offering concludes with forgiveness (sliḥah) — the communal sin offering actually achieves its goal. Unlike the Yom Kippur offering (which achieves annual comprehensive cleansing), each individual sin offering deals with specific instances of unintentional transgression. The formula venislaḥ lo/lahem (he/they shall be forgiven) closes each of the four cases in Leviticus 4.
21: The second bull is burned outside the camp — the same outside-camp disposal as the priestly sin offering (Leviticus 4:12). The legal shorthand ka'asher saraf et-hapar harishon (as he burned the first bull) connects both communal offerings to the established procedure. The outside-camp burning is the defining disposal method for all sin offerings whose blood enters the tent of meeting — the most sin-laden animals go farthest from the center.
22: The third case: the ruler (nasi). The term nasi refers to a tribal or community leader below the priestly and communal level — a chieftain, judge, or prominent figure. His sin offering is a male goat rather than a bull — a significant step down in cost from the priestly/communal bull. The graduated scale of offerings reflects graduated communal impact: a leader's sin affects his followers, but not the whole congregation as the priest's sin does.
23: The ruler's male goat without defect is brought when the sin becomes known (keasher yoda'at ḥatato) — the same awareness-trigger as in the communal case. The phrase underscores that the sin offering system addresses post-hoc discovered violations, not pre-emptive moral cleansing. It is a remedial, not preventive, system.
24: The slaughter location for the ruler's offering is the place for burnt offerings — not inside the tent of meeting. The spatial difference between the leader's offering and the priestly/communal offerings is significant: the blood goes to the outer altar only, not inside the sanctuary. This spatial distinction reflects the lower communal impact of a leader's sin compared to a priest's or congregation's sin.
25: Blood on the horns of the burnt-offering altar only — not the incense altar inside the tent. The blood application goes to the altar in the court, not into the holy place. This is the key spatial distinction between the two levels of sin offering: inner-sanctuary blood manipulation (priest, congregation) versus outer-altar blood manipulation (ruler, common person). The sin offering's spatial penetration reflects the offender's communal weight.
26: The fat portions are burned and atonement is made for the ruler's unintentional sin. The formula closes: venislaḥ lo (he shall be forgiven). The ruler receives the same forgiveness formula as the priest and congregation — the outcome of atonement is uniform across all social levels. Graduated cost structure, uniform forgiveness result.
27: The fourth case: the common person (am ha'arets, lit. 'people of the land' = ordinary Israelite without special status). The common person's sin offering is a female goat — one grade lower than the ruler's male goat. The female offering is less valuable than the male, reflecting the further step down in social and communal impact.
28: The same awareness-trigger: keasher yoda'at ḥatato (when the sin becomes known). The sin-offering system is entirely reactive to discovered violations. The normative assumption is that the person did not know they had sinned; the offering addresses the gap between their ignorance and YHWH's holiness.
29: The common person lays hands and slaughters at the burnt-offering place — same as the ruler. The common person and leader share the outer-altar blood procedure, distinguishing them from the priest/congregation who require inner-sanctuary blood manipulation. The spatial organization of the court reflects the spatial organization of communal accountability.
30: Blood on the horns of the burnt-offering altar — same as the ruler. The four-horn application gives comprehensive coverage of the altar's sanctified points. The horns projecting at each corner were where blood was applied in all altar-horn rituals, creating a four-directional coverage of the altar's sanctified space.
31: Fat removal and altar burning — same as the fellowship offering's fat portions. The reiaḥ niḥoaḥ formula appears here for the sin offering — acceptance language applied to an atoning rather than celebratory offering. The sin offering is received by YHWH as a pleasing act just as the burnt and fellowship offerings are. Forgiveness formula closes: venislaḥ lo.
32: Alternative for the common person: a lamb instead of a female goat. The two options (goat, Leviticus 4:28; lamb, Leviticus 4:32) give the common person flexibility based on what animals they own. The equivalence of goat and lamb for the same offering reflects that species does not matter theologically — what matters is the proper procedure and the defect-free status.
33: The hand-laying and slaughter at the burnt-offering altar — same as Leviticus 4:29-30. The consistent procedure across all variations of the common-person sin offering underlines that the ritual form, not the animal species, carries the atonement meaning.
34: Blood on the horns of the burnt-offering altar and poured out at its base — same as Leviticus 4:30 and 25. The base-pouring of the blood after horn-application is the standard conclusion to the outer-altar blood ritual; the blood not used for horn-application goes to the ground at the altar's base, returning to the earth as the blood-life principle requires.
35: The lamb's fat portions are identified with the fellowship offering lamb's fat — same internal cross-reference technique as Leviticus 4:10 (cattle compared to fellowship offering cattle). The formula closes identically to all other sin-offering cases: fat burned, atonement made, venislaḥ lo (he shall be forgiven). The fourfold repetition of the forgiveness formula across all four social classes emphasizes that the sin offering's purpose — forgiveness — is equally available to the priest, community, leader, and ordinary person. YHWH's atonement provision spans all social levels.
